original_abstract:
Heinrich Bullinger’s *De testamento* (1534) serves as both a theological and historical-biblical treatise, providing a comprehensive framework for his early Reformation thought. Within this work, Bullinger explores a wide array of interconnected themes, including justification by faith, the concept of divine accommodation (the way God adapts his revelation to human capacity), and the central role of the covenant as the primary focus of all Scripture. Expanding on this biblical trajectory, he addresses the original covenant with Adam, the fundamental unity between the Old and New Testaments, the significance of the "seed of Abraham," and the practical implications of the covenant for ethical living. Furthermore, the treatise examines the roles of the Holy Spirit, the Law, the specific conditions and ceremonies of the covenant, and its expression through the sacraments.

Far from being a narrow study of a single doctrine, *De testamento* unveils Bullinger as a sophisticated biblical theologian dedicated to exhorting the elect to live with integrity before the sovereign El Shaddai. A philological analysis of his Latin terminology—specifically *foedus*, *testamentum*, and *pactum*—demonstrates that while Bullinger viewed these terms as technically interchangeable, he deliberately used *pactum* with caution to prevent his readers from projecting medieval "pactum theology" onto his work. Ultimately, Bullinger’s terminological consistency and thematic breadth signal a significant degree of independence from Huldrych Zwingli, establishing him as a distinct and foundational voice in the development of Reformed covenantal thought.
humanized_abstract:
Heinrich Bullinger’s *De testamento* of 1534 is his major theological and historical-biblical contribution to Reformation thought in its early years. A wide range of related issues are dealt with, including justification by faith, divine accommodation of the human situation in revelation, and the central place of the covenant in Scripture. Bullinger then expands on this Scriptural theme by discussing the original covenant with Adam, the unity of the Old and New Testaments, the meaning of the “seed of Abraham,” and the relation of the covenant to ethical living. In addition, Bullinger discusses the role of the Holy Spirit, the Law, the stipulations and commands of the covenant, and the sacraments as signs of the covenant.

In place of being seen as a specialist treatise on one doctrine, De testamento makes Bullinger out to be a thoroughgoing biblical theologian who sought to call the elect to living holy lives before the Lord God El Shaddai. A philological examination of Bullinger’s Latin use of testaments terminology, that is, the words foedus, testamentum, and pactum, reveals that even though Bullinger may have used the terms technically as synonyms, he obviously approached the use of the word pactum with some caution and therefore in order to avoid any possible trace of a medieval pactum-theology in the presentation of his doctrine. In any case Bullinger’s determination in maintaining a consistent terminology, while showing independence of his Zurich colleague Zwingli, at the same time situates him as the major innovator of covenant theology in the Reformed tradition.
